driver.body: The first question in Energy & Charging is not how many chargers Kepler owns but what the estate actually is: **6,180 charging sites across the 42 cities**, carried on the books as either a cost sink or a compounding asset [S-022]. That is not an accounting choice — it turns entirely on where the sites sit, and the placement is precisely where the first cross-domain coupling into the flywheel does its work. The two mechanisms below are that coupling, drilled: siting fed by Mobility's data, then the power economics that a well-sited estate unlocks.

mechanisms.body:
A standalone charge-point operator has one way to decide where capacity goes: it sites against traffic counts and grid-connection availability. That signal is **public, laggy, and available to every competitor at the same moment** — it tells you where demand looked busy last year, not where it will be. Kepler sites against a signal no competitor holds: D1's proprietary dwell-and-dispatch map — where autonomous vehicles actually idle between fares, where riders actually originate, and where the fleet's state-of-charge actually crosses the reorder threshold [S-023]. This is the flywheel's first turn inside the energy domain. Because D1 generates the densest location-and-behaviour data in the network, Kepler can place a fast-charger on the corner where the fleet will need it in 2027, not the corner that looked busy in 2024 [S-201].

The payoff is measured, not asserted. In the **eleven cities where siting has been driven by D1 telemetry rather than public traffic data, first-year utilization ran 31% higher** than in the legacy-sited estate — the single largest driver of site-level payback in the portfolio [S-201]. The mechanism matters because utilization is the numerator of every economic in this domain; a mis-sited charger is not a small loss, it is a stranded asset earning grid-connection charges for a decade with nothing on the other side of the ledger.

The estate itself, rendered as sites × power class × utilization by city, shows why the feed is never generic. In **Jakarta and Ho Chi Minh City the load is dominated by D3's courier fleet on AC chargers**; in **Dubai it is D1's autonomous fleet on DC-fast** [S-201]. The same physical socket serves different domains in different cities — and that is exactly the point. A socket utilised **63% at peak across the network is a socket that two internal customers are paying to keep warm**, a dual-demand profile no single-purpose operator can replicate [S-202]. Siting quality then chains directly into the next mechanism, because a well-sited, dual-fed estate is what makes ownership of the power economics worth having.

component_data.caption: The charging estate — 6,180 sites across 42 cities [S-022] — rendered as sites × power class × demand feed. Bubble size = power class (DC-fast vs AC); the right-hand label is the internal customer paying for the load. D1-sited cities ran first-year utilization +31% over the legacy-sited estate [S-201]; dual-served sockets hit 63% peak utilization, two internal customers per socket [S-202].

mechanisms.body:
A well-sited, dual-fed estate is worth owning up the stack. Because Kepler can forecast aggregate load from the same fleet data behind Mechanism 1.1, it can contract power as an **industrial buyer** — long-dated PPAs, wholesale-market participation, on-site storage — rather than paying retail tariffs at each individual socket [S-024]. The result is a hard number: **blended landed cost of energy across the network is $0.081/kWh against a retail benchmark of $0.163/kWh in the same metros — a 50.3% wholesale-plus discount** that is the raw material of the cost floor [S-203].

This is the mechanism that converts "we own chargers" into "we own the marginal cost of movement." It is the hinge for the entire domain, because that **$0.082/kWh gap is what gets handed down to D1 and D3** — the mobility and logistics fleets that draw on the estate — turning an energy-domain cost advantage into a network-wide one [S-024].
